actions.title:
EL and Academic
Language
Professional
Development
actions.description:
The Office of Accountability will provide professional development to
support English Language Development best practices as well as
academic language strategies to support the English Learner students
attainment of English proficiency and access and progress towards
mastery of all content. In addition, teachers of students who have
redesignated as Fluent English Proficient will also be supported through
strategies that support their continual expansion of language skills in
English.
The Office of Accountability will use the multilingual support team to direct
the use of an observation protocol that informs teachers and administrators
on professional development needs necessary to improve English learner
student outcomes.
To address the needs of our Long Term English Learners, each site will
develop a spreadsheet that will include every LTEL to track their
achievement in ELA and Math. Administrators, TOSAs and teachers will all
have access to this consolidated information to collaborate on strategies to
improve academic outcomes.

actions.title:
EL Academic
Support Services
actions.description:
The Office of Accountability will continue to provide English Learner and
academic support services to improve student achievement to serve our
English learners and students redesignated as Fluent English Proficient.
Including our services provided through our Language Assessment Center.
The Assessment clerks evaluate potential English learners to determine
their ELPAC level. This allows classroom teachers and Instructional
Specialists to create more customized language lessons to accelerate
language proficiency.

actions.title:
EL and Academic
Language
Supplemental
Resources
actions.description:
Based on ongoing analysis of both formative and summative assessment
data, the Accountability department will continue to provide supplemental
resources (including software and technology) in addition to supplemental
services to support our economically disadvantaged students as well as
focus on academic language achievement, principally targeting all profiles
of our English learners (newcomers, long term English learners as well as
students redesignated as Fluent English Proficient.)
Emphasis will be placed on Lampson, Taft, and Villa Park High School to
address placing Red on the EL Progress Indicator on the California
Dashboard.

actions.title:
Professional
Development and
Support for Core
Subjects
actions.description:
The Curriculum department will provide teachers and administrators with
focused and targeted professional development for our diverse learners we
serve that will strengthen their instructional practices and strategies to
ensure success and equitable access for all students in the areas of Math,
Science, English Language Arts, Social Studies, and other content areas,
including scaffolds and support for English learners, students re-
designated as Fluent English Proficient, low socio-economic and foster
youth students.
Emphasis on Foster Youth and the following student groups in OUSD that
received Red in ELA on the California Dashboard:
Long-term English Learners, Foster Youth, Homeless
Emphasis on the following student groups in various schools that received
Red in ELA on the California Dashboard:
Imperial-SWD; Olive-SWD; Palmyra-SWD; Prospect-SWD; Cerro Villa-
English Learners, SWD; Portola-LTELs, SWD; Orange High School-
English Learners, LTELs, SED, SWD, Hispanic; Richland-SED, Hispanic
Emphasis will be placed on subgroups within OUSD (LTELs, Foster Youth,
Homeless) and subgroups within schools (ELMO-English Learners; OHS-
LTELs, SWD; EDGE-SED; Portola-English Learners; Serrano-SWD) that
received red on the Mathematics indicator on the State Dashboard

actions.title:
Early Learning and
Elementary Support
actions.description:
The Early Learning Department will utilize LCFF and state funding sources
t o provide educational opportunities to increase kindergarten readiness for
all students and additional opportunities targeting our multi-lingual, foster
and low-income students. This action will principally support students in
TK.

actions.title: Elementary Support
actions.description:
E lementary Education Department will continue to provide support our our
Elementary level programs that help our youngest students, especially
targeting our English Learners, foster youth and low income students to
build a strong foundation and become better prepared for school. This
includes, instructional assistants at all elementary sites and additional
teachers to reduce combination classrooms at highly impacted sites.
$ 2,249,138 listed in Other State Funds is LREBG funding.

actions.title:
Mathematics Support
Services
actions.description:
The Curriculum Department will provide support services for teachers of
mathematics to enhance their instruction through the incorporation of
standards based practices, academic language instruction, and strategies
for differentiation. Mathematics instructional materials adoption committee
will meet to make recommendations on new K-8 math curriculum. This will
lead to an increase in student achievement for all students, including
English learners, low socioeconomic, and foster youth.
Although primarily targeting secondary, an emphasis will be placed on
subgroups within OUSD (LTELs, Foster Youth, Homeless) and subgroups
within schools (ELMO-English Learners; OHS-LTELs; EDGE-SED;
Portola-English Learners) that received red on the Mathematics indicator
on the State Dashboard

actions.title:
Career Technical
Education
actions.description:
The Curriculum Department will provide students with a pathway to
postsecondary education and careers through Career Technical Education
(CTE) coursework that integrates rigorous core academic knowledge with
technical and occupational knowledge across a variety of Industry Sectors.
CTE teachers will be supported by the Teacher on Special Assignment for
Career Pathways and Inclusive Practices, so they can effectively meet the
needs of English Learners, low socio-economic, and foster youth students.

actions.title:
College and Career
Readiness
actions.description:
The Curriculum department will provide opportunities for students that
prepare them for college and career success, such as differentiation for
advanced learners, AVID, College and Career Fairs, Robotics,
interventions, dual enrollment, summer enrichment, Visual and Performing
Arts (VAPA)programs, STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, and
Math) and Career Technical Education (CTE ) pathway programs. College
and career readiness opportunities will be provided for all learners,
including English learners, low socio-economic, and foster youth students.
Counselors will be provided with training and support so they can
effectively monitor students' college and career readiness outcomes.
Emphasis will be placed on Richland Continuation High School. Richland
was the one school within OUSD that received the lowest performance
level. Specific subgroups at Richland in Red for College and Career
include ALL, EL, LTELs, SED and Hispanic.

actions.title:
GATE (Gifted and
Talented Education)
actions.description:
The Curriculum Department will provide universal testing to consistently
i d entify gifted and talented (GATE) students throughout the district and
professional development for teachers to meet the learning needs of GATE
students.

actions.title:
Multi-Tiered System
of Support (MTSS)
actions.description:
The Office of Student Achievement will collaborate with other district
d epartments and school sites to implement a comprehensive Multi-Tiered
System of Support (MTSS) that incorporates evidence-based best
practices, differentiated learning supports, and universal screening and
progress monitoring. This Multi-Tiered System aims to ensure all students,
including English learners, low socio-economic, and foster youth students,
acquire state academic content standards while addressing academic,
behavioral, and social-emotional needs. Differentiated learning supports
will be provided:
Foster Youth: Specific interventions and supports tailored to the unique
challenges faced by Foster Youth, such as stability issues, trauma-
informed approaches, and targeted counseling or mentoring programs.
EL Students: Differentiated instructional strategies that address language
barriers, such as language acquisition support, and student-centered
teaching practices.
SED Students: Additional academic and social-emotional supports that
consider the impact of socioeconomic factors on learning, including access
to resources, community partnerships for wraparound services, and
targeted interventions to mitigate the effects of poverty.
As part of our Multi-Tiered System of Support, OUSD will provide
resources for elementary school CAST (Collaborative Academic Support
Teams) meetings to monitor student progress, deliver timely interventions
for struggling students, and promote academic and student-centered
wellness.

actions.title: Science Center
actions.description:
The Curriculum Department will provide professional learning and support
for science programs and curriculum, including hands-on exploration using
science kits to make content more meaningful for students, including
English learners, low socio-economic, and foster youth. Using hands-on
science materials allows English learners and students with disabilities to
engage with concepts through direct experience, reducing the language
barrier and enhancing comprehension through practical demonstration.
This multisensory approach helps them connect abstract ideas to tangible
actions, reinforcing learning through active participation.

actions.title: Primary Language
actions.description:
Language Assessment Clerks will administer a language screener to
assess the primary language proficiency in a students primary
language.This will provide the teacher with feedback detailing the level that
that the student is able to speak, read , write and listen in their primary
language.This will support the classroom teacher in determining the proper
scaffold needed for supporting English Language instruction.
Action 1.13 is new in the 2025/26 LCAP. It is Contributing as it supports
English Learners in determining proficiency in their primary language.

actions.title: Foster Youth Support
actions.description:
S t u dent & Community Services provides school materials, including school
supplies, backpacks, and all necessary classroom materials, to Foster
Youth and McKinney-Vento students. All Foster Youth and McKinney-
Vento students will have access to basic school supplies, laptops, and
hotspots, enabling them to access all curricula and establish equity for our
underserved student populations.

actions.title: Assessment Support
actions.description:
T h e Offices of Student Achievement and Curriculum provide support for
student achievement, through implementation of local and state
assessments for all of our students but primarily focusing needed
scaffolding for our English Learners, Foster Youth, and Low-Income
Students.
This action was Contributing in 24/25 because it was specifically focused
on improving achievement and Ed Tech implementation for ELs, Foster
Youth and Low-income students. For 25/26, the action will now apply to all
students (K-2) with the implementation of the reading screener outlined in
EdCode 53008.

actions.title:
Elementary Resource
Teachers
actions.description:
Elementary resource teachers (1 per site) will address the specific needs
of students, each elementary site will have a resource teacher dedicated to
focused, small group instruction. Elementary resource teachers providing
small group instruction offer tailored support that addresses the unique
learning needs of English learners, low-socioeconomic students, and foster
youth, ensuring they receive individualized attention and instruction. This
targeted approach helps close educational gaps, fosters a supportive
learning environment, and promotes academic success by addressing
specific challenges faced by these students.

actions.title:
Visual and
Performing Arts
(VAPA)
actions.description:
The Curriculum Department is committed to enhancing the educational
experience by expanding students' access to visual and performing arts
(VAPA) courses, activities, and resources, including field trips and
instructional materials designed to foster immersive learning experiences
for all students, including English learners, low socio-economic, and foster
youth students. Access to visual and performing arts instruction in school
provides English learners, low socio-economic, and foster youth students
with creative outlets to express themselves, which can enhance their well-
being and communication skills. VAPA instruction helps English learners,
specifically, by providing non-verbal avenues for expression and
comprehension, allowing them to convey ideas and emotions without
relying solely on language proficiency. Additionally, arts activities often
involve collaborative projects and interactive storytelling, which can
improve ELs' vocabulary, listening skills, and confidence in using English in
a supportive and immersive context.

actions.title:
Secondary Academic
Support
actions.description:
The Secondary Education Department will expand access to instructional
materials and courses that provide powerful learning experiences for all
students including Advanced Placement (AP), International Baccalaureate
Program (IB), and rigorous college and career STEM courses, as well as
intervention and targeted academic support services, which includes credit
recovery, summer school, extended learning, AVID, Dual Language and
enrichment to primarily meet the needs of our low income, English learners
and foster youth.

actions.title: P21 Specialists
actions.description:
The Office of Secondary Education will continue to provide P21 specialists
to all comprehensive high schools to coordinate CTE pathway
development and STEM opportunities for students with a special emphasis
on our English learners, foster youth and low income students to ensure
they are pathway completers.

actions.title:
Special Education
Instruction
Specialists
actions.description:
Our Special Education Department will provide Instructional Specialist
support to Special Education teachers to lead implementation of
curriculum, engagement strategies and specialized techniques to support
our students with exceptional needs. In addition to ongoing annual ongoing
teacher support, the IS team provides on-demand coaching for school
teams, as part of the Exemplary Educator Initiative.

actions.title:
Innovative Learning-
Educational
Technology
actions.description:
Technology Instruction and Innovation Labs - The Office of Educational
Technology is partnering through a grant with Verizon to provide two
coaches to serve at two of our middle schools with a high number of low-
income students in supporting 1:1 technology integration for every staff
and student. The coaches will assist the instructional staff in raising the
quality of instruction using the latest technology in the classrooms and
further developing the Innovation Labs that support students developing
project-based learning curricula tied to STEM careers.
Computer Science and Math Instruction - A resource math teacher will
support the expansion of the C-STEM program throughout OrangeUSD
(focusing on integrating robotics with mathematics). The resource teacher
directly supports students and teachers to expand computer science
pathways tied to mathematics instruction in K-12 classrooms.
STEM Pathways - Project Lead the Way - Expansion of the hands-on
program PLTW will support pathways between elementary, middle, and
high school. A resource specialist will work directly to support the
professional development of teachers and classroom demonstrations. A
pathway will be completed for each high school over a 4 year period that
provides training and curriculum for schools.
Artificial Intelligence - Research and create professional development
around supporting OrangeUSD staff and community in AI ethical use within
their roles throughout the school year. Professional Development will be
constructed around the district-developed OrangeUSD Guidelines that will
focus on providing AI Resources, Software, and Best practices for Staff
and Students.

actions.title:
Student Achievement
Data Analysis,
Progress Monitoring
Software, Student
Achievement
Committee, and
Student Achievement
Professional
Learning
actions.description:
The Office of Student Achievement will foster and support innovative
programs to meet the needs of students. These learning opportunities,
data analysis and monitoring software and programs will enhance the
learnings on specific subgroups, English Learners, Foster Youth, and Low
Income.

actions.title: STEAM Education
actions.description:
The Office of Educational Technology will continue to build a positive and
supportive culture to provide comprehensive STEAM (Science,
Technology, Engineering, Arts, and Math) education with a focus on high-
quality mathematics instruction and career connections that meet the
targeted needs of all students, with a primary focus on our English
learners, low income and foster youth, to support increases in academic
achievement and preparation for career readiness.

actions.title:
Professional
Learning Plan
actions.description:
Educational Technology will develop a long-term targeted and transparent
professional learning plan that aligns with district goals, enhances technical
skills, and provides technology-based instruction strategies for all teachers
supporting all students to track skill growth year to year and determine the
frequency of technology use for instruction. This action is principally
directed to EL, LI and Foster Youth students.

actions.title:
Student Achievement
and Ed. Tech
Support
actions.description:
The offices of Student Achievement and Educational Technology will
provide personnel to support OUSD Student Achievement with a focus on
improving student achievement and enhancing the use of Educational
Technology specifically for our English Learners, Foster Youth and Low
Income Students.

actions.title:
Engaging, Rigorous
and Student
Centered Learning
Environments
actions.description:
The Curriculum department provides Teachers on Special Assignment
(TOSA) to support teachers across the district through professional
development and in-class coaching on engaging rigorous and student
centered learning environments. Thus, all our targeted subgroups,
including our English Learner, foster youth and low incomes students are
given opportunities to unlock their potential so that they can achieve
successful outcomes.

actions.title:
Expanded Learning
and Summer
Enrichment
actions.description:
The Expanded Learning Department will provide funding for afterschool
and summer school enrichment programs for kindergarten (including TK)
through sixth grade to promote high levels of student engagement and
wellness, with an emphasis in strong student social emotional support and
safety protocols.

actions.title:
Increase Attendance
and Enrollment Rates
actions.description:
Student and Community Services will deploy dedicated staff across the
d i s t rict to lead attendance initiatives, providing outreach and emphasizing
the importance of daily attendance through school events, community
engagement, and targeted communication. We will offer ongoing
professional development for teachers, support staff, and families to build
capacity in attendance monitoring and early intervention.
In collaboration with local agencies and community partners, we will
expand support services to reduce barriers and improve student
engagement. Additionally, a centralized attendance-tracking system will be
implemented at all school sites to enhance data sharing, identify chronic
absenteeism early, and coordinate timely, site-based responses.
With a strong focus on our unduplicated student groups, low-income
students, foster youth, and English learners, these efforts aim to close
achievement gaps, support social-emotional well-being, and ensure that
every student benefits from consistent, meaningful participation in school.
Emphasis will be placed on schools in OUSD and student groups within a
school that received Red in the area of Chronic Absenteeism:
Cerro Villa-English Learners, Hispanic, 2 or more races
The subgroup African American received Red in chronic absenteeism for
OUSD on the California Dashboard. Subgroup to watch that received
Orange is Foster Youth.

actions.title:
Student and
Community Services
Administrator
actions.description:
Student & Community Services will continue to be led by an Executive
Director who oversees district-wide child welfare, attendance, community
engagement, and school connectedness, and provides leadership in core
district initiatives, including safety and security, student well-being and
support, and student services and compliance.

actions.title:
Intramural Sports and
Athletics
actions.description:
The Office of Secondary Education will continue to fund intramural sports
and athletics to eliminate barriers and to allow continued engagement of all
students, including EL, LI and FY.

actions.title:
Additional Health
Supports
actions.description:
Student & Community Services is dedicated to ensuring equitable access
to healthcare and support for every student, predominantly our low-income,
English learner, and foster youth populations, while championing the health
and well-being of our entire school community.

actions.title:
Student Intervention
Resources and
Support
actions.description:
Student and Community Services is committed to providing timely health
interventions and personalized support to every student. By partnering
year-round with community organizations, we harness their expertise to
meet the needs of our most vulnerable populations, including McKinney-
Vento, foster youth, low-income, and English learner students.

goals.description:
Condition & Climate: Orange Unified maintains strong conditions and climates that directly support
efficient and effective teaching and learning environments for our students and staff as well as a
welcoming atmosphere for our families and our community.

actions.title:
School site
allocations
actions.description:
The Office of Accountability, in conjunction with our Budget department,
collaborate on developing formulas for school site allocations so that
school site leaders have resources
available to improve student outcomes, increase academic achievement
and close learning gaps for English Learners, Foster Youth and Low
Income students. Based on each site's unique needs, the funds are used
for a variety of actions to meet needs of their student population. School
sites use LCFF funds for community liaisons who work directly with
families of English learners, low income and and foster youth students to
build relationships to foster connectedness with the school. These liaisons
provide resources to health services, food and clothing to support families
in need. They also assist with the site ELAC and provide parent education
under the direction of the district TOSA of Parent and Family Engagement.
Sites utilize LCFF funds for other supplemental personnel like resource
teachers to provide additional academic support to unduplicated students
to bridge the achievement gap caused by lack of exposure, limited English
language, and other factors. Tutoring is provided afterschool to remediate
gaps in essential skills that cause these students to perform below
benchmark levels. Often, lack of English contributes to this
underperformance; thus, tutoring also supports English language
acquisition. Our sites use LCFF funds to purchase supplemental supplies
and equipment, particularly to support AVID. Sites also provide parent and
family engagement in addition to that provided by the community liaison to
provide specific content related enrichment such as Science Night and
Math Night. These workshops educate parents and families on standards
and content that students are exposed to so that they can support their
child(ren) at home. Sites share their plans for using the LCFF funds
through the School Plan for Student Achievement in their continuous
improvement cycle process. The SPSA is a collaborative effort between
school leadership, School Site Council and ELAC. Principals continually
address actions in the SPSA and document any changes discussed in the
minutes for SSC and as part of the annual program evaluation of the
school plan, they will analyze the implementation of the actions for
effectiveness and share with SSC and ELAC as well as document within
the school plan. The SPSA is taken annually to our Board of Education for
adoption.

actions.title:
Administrative
Support for
Categorical
Programs
actions.description:
The Office of Accountability manages the categorically funded programs
that support low income, English Learners and foster youth district wide.
The department interfaces and collaborates with all school sites and our
budget, assessment and technology departments to ensure services are
provided and implemented with the lens of equity and fidelity to ensure
positive student outcomes.

actions.title:
Foster Youth
Coordinator
actions.description:
The SCS Coordinator provides direct, tailored support for our Foster Youth,
collaborating with students, families, and school teams to ensure equitable
access to resources and opportunities. Key services include campus
tutoring, the College Connections Program (offering FAFSA assistance
and graduation tracking), and meet-and-greet events with local college
representatives to facilitate the transition to postsecondary education. The
Coordinator also manages the Connections Mentor program, ensuring that
all Foster Youth and McKinney-Vento students can participate in
extracurricular activities and receive wraparound support. These efforts,
guided by our Differentiated Assistance model, target the specific
academic and social-emotional needs of our students who require the most
support.
Based on Differentiated Assistance, this action supports addressing our
Foster Youth population in OUSD, which received the lowest performance
level on one or more indicators (Suspension, ELA, Math).

actions.title:
Foster Youth and
McKinney-Vento
Mentors
actions.description:
Student & Community Services (SCS) provides Connections Mentors who
support Foster Youth by promoting academic success, school
engagement, and access to resources. Mentors offer one-on-one or small
group sessions, connect students with on-campus support, and help those
with Ds and Fs improve their performance. Mentors are regularly trained
and play a crucial role in fostering a sense of belonging. SCS also leads
the Foster Connections Program, offering quarterly sessions that connect
foster parents and caregivers with school staff and community resources.
These initiatives address performance gaps among Foster Youth,
particularly in suspension rates, ELA, and math, as identified through
Differentiated Assistance.
Based on Differentiated Assistance, this action supports addressing our
Foster Youth population in OUSD, which received the lowest performance
level on one or more indicators (ELA, Math).

actions.title:
Safe and Welcoming
Schools
actions.description:
Student and Community Services is dedicated to strengthening
professional development by providing training, coaching, and networking
opportunities focused on school safety, emergency response, threat
assessment, and tiered intervention strategies. These efforts address
critical issues, including chronic absenteeism, behavior management, and
alternatives to suspension. A key focus is supporting increased
engagement among our most vulnerable student populations, including
low-income students, English learners, and foster youth.
Emphasis will be placed on schools in OUSD and student groups within a
school that received RED in the area of Chronic Absenteeism:
Cerro Villa-English Learners, Hispanic, 2 or more races
This action will support schools in OUSD that received RED in Suspension;
however based on the 2024 Dashboard, there are no schools or subgroups
in schools in RED

actions.title: Counseling Services
actions.description:
I n partnership with the Office of Secondary Education, Student &
Community Services helps lead and support school counselors across all
grade levels. Counselors provide academic guidance, social-emotional
learning, and college and career readiness support, including classroom
lessons and targeted check-ins, to foster youth and low-income students.
When students' needs extend beyond school support, counselors work
with site administrators to connect families with additional resources. The
Counselor on Special Assignment (COSA) plays a crucial role in
reengagement efforts, participating in School Attendance Review Board
(SARB) hearings, supporting students after suspension, and coordinating
referrals for crisis counseling and substance education services.

actions.title:
Special Education
Programs, Supports,
Staff and Supplies
actions.description:
The Office of Special Education will provide interventions and student
supports through multi-tiered system of support through personnel such as
administrators, educational specialists, psychologists, related service
providers and instructional assistants. Additionally, through the community
assistant at OPK, the department is serving the needs of our parent
community. The community assistant works to engage parents of students
with exceptional needs through phone and email contacts regarding events
and meetings. The department is committed to the ongoing professional
growth of our staff through best practice trainings, along with providing
tools to better support the needs of students with exceptional needs. Our
special education works in tandem with our families to collaborate on
individualized education programs that provide the services, materials and
programs that are essential to meet every student's needs to access their
education. This action is measured by metrics 3.9 and 3.10. $425,726
from Other State Funds represents LREBG funding.
This action addresses our SPED population who are identified as lowest
performing in a math and/or ELA at Cerro Villa Middle, Imperial, Olive,
Orange High, Palmyra, Portola Middle, Prospect, Serrano

actions.title:
Technology:
Infrastructure,
Devices
actions.description:
Technology Services will provide training, infrastructure, and infrastructure
support for staff and students. Technology Services provides maintains the
camera system, Voice-over-IP phone system, and infrastructure. The
department maintains software systems and staffing to maintain an
assigned device for each staff and student within OUSD.
The network of 1,800 access points, 600 network switches.
IT continues to support the financial, HR, and Student Information Systems
(SIS) as well as implement data integration and analytics services for these
platforms.
Safety and Security
The Department of Technology Services will further develop safety
protocols with a multi-department approach to all school sites to increase
safe online practices by teachers and students to protect data privacy.
Cybersecurity training is provided to employees to ensure the safety and
security of student data and infrastructure.
Technology Refresh Program
The district maintains the Technology Refresh Program to provide each
student with an iPad for grades K-2 or 14" HD Windows laptop for 3rd
grade and above to support personalized learning and access to
knowledge. Furthermore, devices are provided to designated positions to
ensure a reliable and consistent computing platform to serve students and
the community. Student devices are refreshed in a three or four-year cycle,
whereas staff devices are refreshed every five years.

actions.title: Textbooks
actions.description:
The Curriculum Department will purchase TK-12th grade textbooks and
instructional materials that are aligned with, and will provide access for all
students to, California's academic and performance state standards
(including a new K-8 math adoption, additional intervention, dual
immersion materials, CTE materials and consumable replacement
purchases).

actions.title:
Teacher Preparation
and Support
actions.description:
The Curriculum Department will provide an accredited California Teacher
Induction Program (CTIP) for beginning general education and special
education teachers, and teacher preparation support for Career Technical
Education (CTE) and intern teachers to ensure every student in Orange
Unified is served by a teacher who is prepared to create effective learning
environments, plan and implement learning experiences to equitably meet
student needs, including English learners, low socio-economic, and foster
youth students. Curriculum Department will also provide a California Clear
Administrative Services Credential program for new administrators to
enhance leadership effectiveness and the ability to support student
achievement through informed decision-making.

actions.title:
Communication
Tools and Resources
actions.description:
The Office of Information Technology will provide tools, forums and
r e s ources, including a district wide communication platform to inform and
engage our diverse community of educational partners. All printed material
is translated to Spanish to support communication with families.
Communication with our school community supports engagement,
principally directed to our EL, LI and FI who are identified as RED at many
sites in Chronic Absenteeism.
This action was Contributing in the 2024/25 LCAP, but has been changed
to All Students for the 2025/26 LCAP. The district is changing the
communication platform from School Messenger, a tool that was primarily
used for communication with our Spanish speaking families. Based on
input from our educational partners, there was a need for a new platform
that will be used by all schools districtwide. Beginning in 2025, all schools
will use the new platform for all communication.

actions.title:
High Quality
Educators and Staff
actions.description:
Orange Unified School District will provide high quality, effective educators
i n every classroom, as well as site and District administrators and support
staff districtwide to ensure that every student and staff are provided an
excellent learning environment each and every day.
$371, 018 of Other State Funds represents LREBG funding.

actions.title: Climate Survey
actions.description:
The Office of Student Achievement will administer a school climate survey
to students to students in grades 6, 8, and 12 to obtain their perspective on
school connectedness, overall school climate, and how OUSD fosters a
supportive and inclusive learning environment.

actions.title:
Parent and Family
Engagement
actions.description:
The Office of Accountability will provide support to increase engagement of
parents and families of EL, LI and FY through resources, software,
trainings, workshops conferences, consultants and stipends for
interpretation.

actions.title: LCAP Survey
actions.description:
The Office of Student Achievement will support all costs associated with
the annual LCAP community partner engagement process which involves
staff, parents, community and students in order to inform our Local Control
and Accountability Plan to provide more equitable opportunities and
outcomes for our students, with special attention to our English Learner,
foster youth and low income students. Questions align with our Strategic
Plan and center on actions, services and programs offered in OUSD.

actions.title:
Maintain
Infrastructure:
Business, Human
Resources,
Maintenance,
Operations and
Transportation
actions.description:
Orange Unified School District will ensure that all campuses will be clean
and safe learning and working environments and they will use the results
of the annual Facilities Inspection Tool (FIT) and the common trends from
participants for facility improvement in our LCAP survey to address facility
and maintenance needs at various school sites annually. In addition,
Orange Unified School District will also provide safe and reliable
transportation to and from school and other events so that transportation is
never an issue for our students.
$3,850,706 of Other State Funds represents LREBG funding.
This action was Action 3.24 in the 2024/25 LCAP and was Not
Contributing. For 2025/26, this action now includes the addition of all
funding sources to support Human Resources and Business. It continues
to be for All Students and Not Contributing.

actions.title:
Parent iPad
Academy
actions.description:
The Office of Accountability has developed and implemented the Parent
iPad Academy to provide parents of English learners the opportunity to
engage with applications on a district purchased iPad to become more
knowledgeable with technology and applications that support family
wellness and student academic progress.

actions.title: Interpreter
actions.description:
The Office of Accountability will provide interpretation and translation for
our Spanish-speaking parents and families of unduplicated pupils to
ensure equitable access to information, materials, meetings and
presentations for families of our English Learners.

actions.title:
Early Learning
Partnerships
actions.description:
The Early Learning Department will partner with community organizations
to provide funding to support families with children Birth to Four through
family education, resources and engaged learning opportunities daily
through OUSD.
Early Learning Partnerships was Action 3.22 in the 2024/25 LCAP. The
action did not change, only the number. It was Not Contributing in 24/25
and remains the same for 25/26.

actions.title:
Support for EL and
Low Income Families
actions.description:
The Office of Accountability provides forums for our English Learner and
L o w Income families and caregivers to be informed of the services,
programs and resources available in our district and in the surrounding
community. We also provide meetings to communicate to site staff
responsible for connecting them to their community or district resources.

actions.title:
Community Schools,
including Native
American Students
and Families
actions.description:
The Office of Accountability will support the efforts of Community Schools
through professional development of Community Leads, establishing
partnerships with community organizations, and development and
implementation of workshops and resources that engage parents and
families throughout the community, principally supporting EL, LI and Foster
Youth students. The Title VI Native American grant additionally supports
Native American students throughout our community with these workshops
and resources specifically for native students.

goals.description:
Equity Multiplier Funds: Within three years, all students, and particularly low-income, EL, and
Hispanic students, at Richland Continuation High School and our OUSD Community Day School,
will demonstrate growth towards meeting or exceeding standards in Math as measured by CAASPP
test results (Richland only) and local benchmark assessments. In addition, the percentage of EL
students making progress toward English language proficiency will exceed 14.5% achieved in 2023.
The percentage of all students including low-income, EL and Hispanic students prepared for college
and career will exceed the .8% from 2023.

actions.title:
Support for English
Learners
actions.description:
Richland will receive support for their English Learners from an EL TOSA.
The TOSA will provide professional development in GLAD for the teachers
and participate in observations to support the process. Specifics are
included in Richland's SPSA. Funds to support this are delineated in the
school SPSA.

actions.title:
Support to Increase
Student Engagement
and Reduce Chronic
Absenteeism at
Richland
actions.description:
Richland will collaborate with Santiago Community College to implement a
dual enrollment class that will serve to engage students and encourage
attendance.

actions.title:
Academic and
Wellness Support for
CDS and Richland
actions.description:
EL, LI and Foster Youth Students at Community Day School need
additional supports, especially in the area of emotional wellness. Richland
w ill utilize additional counseling hours to specifically address attendance.

actions.title: Math Support
actions.description:
Students at Richland scored very low on the California Dashboard in Math.
An additional section to allow for co-teaching in math to increase math
scores.
